title: Coherent spectroscopy of collective excitations in superfluid helium far from equilibrium
authors: Gabriel Voith, Alexander A. Milner, Valery Milner
abstract: Ultrafast dynamics of collective excitations in superfluids remains largely unexplored beyond the roton regime, despite its importance for understanding nonequilibrium processes in these systems. Here, we employ ultrafast coherent control with sequences of femtosecond pulses to perform spectroscopy of multiple branches of the Landau excitation spectrum in superfluid helium far from equilibrium. By measuring the time-resolved optical birefringence, we track the nonequilibrium dynamics of maxon pairs and Pitaevskii plateau excitations alongside the previously studied roton pairs, revealing surprisingly strong binding energy of maxon pairs, their extremely short lifetime, and the influence of the quasiparticle effective mass on the phase of the coherent response. These results demonstrate the ability to extract previously inaccessible information about collective excitations in a strongly interacting quantum fluid by probing its nonequilibrium dynamics on femtosecond and picosecond timescales.
summary: This paper uses ultrafast laser pulses to perform spectroscopy on collective excitations in superfluid helium far from equilibrium. By using a 'double-kick' method to suppress the dominant roton signal, the authors successfully identified and characterized previously inaccessible maxon and Pitaevskii plateau excitations. The study reveals new insights into the binding energy, lifetime, and phase dynamics of these quasiparticles.
main_problem: Exploring the ultrafast, non-equilibrium dynamics of collective excitations in superfluid helium beyond the well-studied roton regime, specifically identifying maxon and Pitaevskii plateau excitations which are obscured by the dominant roton response.
main_result: The researchers successfully isolated maxon pairs and Pitaevskii plateau excitations using coherent control, revealing that maxon pairs have a surprisingly large binding energy and an extremely short lifetime compared to rotons.
method: Ultrafast coherent control using sequences of femtosecond infrared pump pulses and a frequency-doubled probe pulse to measure time-resolved optical birefringence via a Michelson interferometer.
model_or_system: Superfluid Helium-4 at 1.4 K and saturated vapor pressure, studied in a far-from-equilibrium regime using an optical cryostat.
key_observables: Time-dependent optical birefringence, power spectrum of the signal, and the spectral phase of the excitations.
important_parameters: Pulse duration of ~70-100 fs, dynamics on picosecond timescales, frequencies in the GHz range (~350-680 GHz), and maxon decay constant of ~3 ps.
main_assumptions: The birefringence signal can be modeled as a superposition of decaying sinusoidal components representing different quasiparticle branches.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the experimental setup; Fig 2 displays time-dependent birefringence and its numerical fit; Fig 3 presents the power spectrum and spectral phase identifying R, M, and P peaks; Fig 4 shows the suppression of rotons via a 'double kick' and the resulting residual spectrum; Fig 5 shows the isolated maxon signal contribution.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of studying non-equilibrium excitations, describes the experimental setup and coherent control method, presents the identification of maxon and plateau features through signal suppression, analyzes the energy and phase properties of these excitations, and concludes with the implications for studying strongly interacting quantum fluids.
why_interesting: This work demonstrates advanced coherent control techniques to isolate weak many-body excitations from a dominant background, which is highly relevant for studying non-equilibrium dynamics and quasiparticle interactions in any strongly interacting quantum system.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Diagram of the experimental setup. Femtosecond pulses with the central wavelength of 798 nm (upper, red) and 399 nm (lower, blue) serve as the pump and probe, respectively. The pulses are linearly polarized at 45◦with respect to one another, combined in a collinear geometry and focused into the bulk liquid helium, kept at the temperature of 1.4 K and saturated vapor pressure (SVP). A Michelson interferometer splits pump pulses into pairs with a variable delay τ and relative amplitude. P: polarizer, M: mirror, A: variable attenuator, BS: beam splitter, DM: dichroic mirror, L:lens, λ/2, λ/4: zero-order half- and quarter-wave plates.

Figure 2 shows the time-dependent optical birefrin- gence of superfluid helium induced by a single femtosec- ond pump pulse. The oscillatory signal is dominated by the two-roton response around 350 GHz, consistent with our earlier observations [12]. As reported previ- ously, the instantaneous frequency of this signal is not constant but changes on a picosecond timescale. Our re- cent observation that the signal scales linearly with the pump pulse energy [18], with the initial roton frequency remaining unchanged, suggests that the frequency evolu- tion is not driven by laser-induced temperature changes. Instead, we show that the roton signal can be described very well by a superposition of...

FIG. 2. Laser-induced optical birefringence of superfluid he- lium at T = 1.4 K and saturated vapor pressure (solid red line). The insets demonstrate the quality of a numerical fit (dashed black) at both early and late times (upper and lower inset, respectively) by a three-frequency model consisting of two roton components and one maxon component (see text for details).

FIG. 3. Power spectrum (solid red, log scale) and the spectral phase (dashed blue) of the time-dependent signal shown in Fig. 2. The three pronounced peaks correspond to rotons, maxons, and Pitaevskii plateau, and are labeled with ‘R’, ‘M’, and ‘P’, respectively.

FIG. 4. (a) Birefringence signal (red solid line) recorded after the excitation by two pump pulses, separated in time by τ = 1.45 ps (as indicated with two vertical arrows). For reference, the response to the second pulse alone (analogous to that in Fig. 2) is shown by the dashed black curve. The power spectrum of the residual signal is shown in panel (b), with the roton (‘R’), maxon (‘M’), and Pitaevskii plateau (‘P’) peaks labeled accordingly.
